_comment: Dreizunge v37 prompt templates. Use {L} for target language name, {S} for source language name, {diff} for difficulty label, {sentLen} for sentence length spec, {lessonDiff} for lesson focus label. Conditional fragments are assembled in server.js.

vocab.dialectNote: - The target language variety is: {dialect}. Use vocabulary and spelling appropriate to this dialect/variety.
vocab.cjkNote: - JAPANESE: In sentence "target" fields, annotate each kanji character with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Only annotate kanji (CJK characters) — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.user:
Topic: "{topic}". Lesson {lessonNum} of {totalLessons}.
Target language: {L}. Source/translation language: {S}.
vocab.storyHint_source:
Context story (in {S} — the learner's native language — for theme context only. Do NOT copy its vocabulary into "source" fields; generate fresh {S} translations):
{storyExcerpt}
vocab.prevHint:
Vocabulary already covered in earlier lessons (do NOT repeat these):
{prevVocab}
vocab.chainHint:
This is a continuation lesson. REINFORCE these words from previous chapters by using them naturally in sentences (do NOT just repeat them as vocab items):
{chainVocab}
vocab.srcCjkNote: - JAPANESE SOURCE: The source/translation language is Japanese. Annotate each kanji in "source" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.cjkNote1: - JAPANESE BEGINNER: Annotate EVERY kanji in "target" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Annotate all kanji without exception — the learner cannot read any kanji yet. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.cjkNote2: - JAPANESE: In sentence "target" fields, annotate each kanji with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.cjkNote3: - JAPANESE ADVANCED: Annotate only less common or complex kanji in "target" fields with readings in square brackets (e.g. 語彙[ごい]). Skip N4/N5 level kanji such as 日、本、人、大、小、山、川、水、火、木 etc. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.srcCjkNote1: - JAPANESE SOURCE BEGINNER: Annotate EVERY kanji in "source" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Annotate all kanji without exception. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.srcCjkNote2: - JAPANESE SOURCE: The source/translation language is Japanese. Annotate each kanji in "source" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocab.srcCjkNote3: - JAPANESE SOURCE ADVANCED: Annotate only less common or complex kanji in "source" fields with readings in square brackets. Skip N4/N5 kanji. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.

vocabFromText.cjkNote: - JAPANESE: In sentence "target" fields, annotate each kanji character with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Only annotate kanji (CJK characters) — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabFromText.prevHint:
Vocabulary already used in earlier lessons (avoid repeating these):
{prevVocab}
vocabFromText.chainHint:
This is a continuation lesson. REINFORCE these words from previous chapters:
{chainVocab}
vocabFromText.srcCjkNote: - JAPANESE SOURCE: The source/translation language is Japanese. Annotate each kanji in "source" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabFromText.cjkNote1: - JAPANESE BEGINNER: Annotate EVERY kanji in "target" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Annotate all kanji without exception — the learner cannot read any kanji yet. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabFromText.cjkNote2: - JAPANESE: In sentence "target" fields, annotate each kanji with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabFromText.cjkNote3: - JAPANESE ADVANCED: Annotate only less common or complex kanji in "target" fields with readings in square brackets (e.g. 語彙[ごい]). Skip N4/N5 level kanji such as 日、本、人、大、小、山、川、水、火、木 etc. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabFromText.srcCjkNote1: - JAPANESE SOURCE BEGINNER: Annotate EVERY kanji in "source" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Annotate all kanji without exception. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabFromText.srcCjkNote2: - JAPANESE SOURCE: The source/translation language is Japanese. Annotate each kanji in "source" fields with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.

grammar.system:
You are a {L} grammar lesson generator for a vocabulary learning app.
The learner speaks {S} and is learning {L}.
Return ONLY a valid JSON object — no markdown, no explanation, nothing else.

Generate a grammar lesson focused on noun gender, articles, and plural forms.
Schema:
{
  "title": "short lesson theme in {S} (3-5 words)",
  "desc": "up to 8 words in {S}",
  "icon": "one relevant emoji",
  "grammar": [
    {
      "target": "{L} noun in singular form (no article)",
      "source": "{S} translation of that noun",
      "gender": "m | f | n | c (common) — grammatical gender in {L}",
      "article": "definite article in {L} for this noun",
      "plural": "plural form of the noun in {L}"
    }
  ]
}

Example (German ← French):
{"target":"Baum","source":"arbre","gender":"m","article":"der","plural":"Bäume"}

CRITICAL field language rules — violations will cause the lesson to be discarded:
- CRITICAL: "target" MUST contain ONLY a {L} word — never {S}, never the translation
- CRITICAL: "source" MUST contain ONLY the {S} translation — never {L}
- CRITICAL: "target" and "source" MUST be different words (never the same string)
- CRITICAL: "target" MUST be the SINGULAR form — never already a plural
- CRITICAL: "plural" MUST differ from "target" (if they are the same, choose a different noun)
- CRITICAL: "article" MUST be the correct {L} definite article for the SINGULAR noun
- "target" must have no article prepended — singular noun only

Additional rules:
- grammar: exactly 10 nouns, varied difficulty: {diff}, all topic-relevant
- Include nouns with interesting/irregular plurals and mixed genders
- If {L} has no grammatical gender (e.g. English), set gender and article to null
grammar.user:
Topic: "{topic}". Target language: {L}. Source language: {S}.
Generate 10 {L} nouns with gender, article, and plural forms. "target" fields in {L}, "source" fields in {S}. Return only the JSON object.
grammar.storyHint: Context nouns/themes from the story (prefer these where topic-relevant): {storyKeywords}
grammar.priorHint: PRIOR NOUNS from previous chapters (reinforce where topic-relevant): {priorNouns}

conjugation.user:
Topic: "{topic}". Target language: {L}. Source language: {S}.
Generate 5 {L} verbs with all present-tense conjugation forms. "infinitive" fields in {L}, "source" fields in {S}. Return only the JSON object.
conjugation.storyHint: Context verbs/themes from the story (prefer these where topic-relevant): {storyKeywords}
conjugation.priorHint: PRIOR VERBS from previous chapters (reinforce where topic-relevant): {priorVerbs}

errorHunt.system:
You are a language exercise generator for {L} learners.
Given a {L} story, produce a corrupted version by introducing exactly {nSpell} spelling errors and {nGrammar} grammar errors.

SPELLING errors — change only an interior letter of one word:
- swap one letter for a similar-looking/sounding one (e.g. ei→ie, b→d)
- omit or double one interior letter (e.g. "manger"→"mnager")
- use a wrong vowel that sounds plausible (e.g. "o"→"u")
- wrong or missing accent/diacritic (e.g. "é"→"e")
The corrupted word must NOT be a prefix or suffix extension of the correct word.

GRAMMAR errors — add or remove a suffix on one word:
- wrong verb suffix for the subject (e.g. "explore"→"explores", "mangiano"→"mangia")
- wrong plural/singular (e.g. "cell"→"cells", "cats"→"cat")
- wrong tense suffix (e.g. "walk"→"walked", "oscillated"→"oscillate")
The correct word must be a prefix of the corrupted word or vice versa.

STRICT RULES — violations will cause the lesson to be rejected:
- Change ONLY a single complete word per error — never punctuation, numbers, proper nouns, or sentence-start capitals
- The correct word must appear verbatim in the original story
- The correct and corrupted words MUST be different
- Do NOT produce no-op changes (e.g. adding/removing only spaces or punctuation)
- Do NOT change more than one word per error
- Copy all other text EXACTLY — do not rephrase, reorder, or rewrite anything

Return ONLY the corrupted story text. No JSON, no explanation, no markdown.

translation.system: You are a professional translator. Translate the following {L} text into natural, fluent {S}. Translate sentence by sentence in the same order. Output only the {S} translation — no explanations, no original text, no markdown.

meta.system:
You are a lesson plan creator. Return ONLY a valid JSON object, no markdown, no explanation.
Schema: {"topic":"display title (max 40 chars, concise)","topicEmoji":"one emoji"}
CRITICAL: The "topic" display title MUST be written in {S}. Do not use any other language.
Rules: topic is a concise display title for the user's input — shorten if the input is longer than 40 characters.

story.system: You are a creative writer and {L} language teacher. Write a short story directly in {L} on the given topic — aim for {paraLo}-{paraHi} paragraphs, under {wc} words. Prioritise quality over length: stop early rather than pad or repeat. Never repeat a sentence or paragraph in altered form. Avoid repetitive structures. Plain prose only — no headings, no bullets, no markdown. Write entirely in {L}.
story.furiganaNote: Annotate every kanji with its reading in square brackets directly after, e.g. 日本語[にほんご]. Only annotate kanji (CJK characters) — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
story.continuationNote: IMPORTANT: This is a continuation story. You will be given the previous story. Continue with the SAME characters, world, and narrative thread — but shift the topic and setting to the new one. Characters should feel familiar; the world connected.
story.furiganaNote1: Annotate EVERY kanji with its reading in square brackets directly after, e.g. 日本語[にほんご]. Annotate all kanji without exception — the learner cannot read any kanji yet. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
story.furiganaNote2: Annotate every kanji with its reading in square brackets directly after, e.g. 日本語[にほんご]. Only annotate kanji (CJK characters) — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
story.furiganaNote3: Annotate only less common or complex kanji with readings in square brackets, e.g. 語彙[ごい]. Skip common N4/N5 kanji. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.

storylineTitle.system: You are a creative writing assistant. Given a multi-chapter story, produce a single JSON object with exactly two fields: "title" (a short evocative series title, 2–5 words) and "icon" (one emoji that fits the story). The "title" MUST be written in {S}. Return ONLY the JSON object, no markdown, no explanation.

storylineSummary.system: You are a language learning assistant. Given a multi-chapter story, write a concise summary in {S} (3–5 sentences) that captures the main arc and key vocabulary themes. Write ONLY the summary text — no title, no preamble, no markdown.

vocabTable.system:
You are a {L} language lesson creator.
The learner speaks {S} and is learning {L}.
Create lesson {lessonNum} of {totalLessons} focused on {lessonDiff} at {diff} level.
Output TWO markdown tables and nothing else.

Table 1 — Vocabulary (exactly 8 rows):
| {L} word | {S} meaning | Pronunciation for {S} speakers |
|---|---|---|
| word in {L} | translation in {S} | phonetic pronunciation |

Table 2 — Sentences (exactly 5 rows):
| {L} sentence | {S} translation |
|---|---|
| sentence in {L} | translation in {S} |

Rules:
- Column 1 ({L} content): MUST be genuine {L} — never {S} or another language
- Column 2 ({S} content): MUST be in {S} — never {L} or English (unless {S} is English)
- Pronunciation: write phonetically for {S} speakers, CAPS for stressed syllable
- No extra text, no headings outside the tables, no JSON
vocabTable.cjkNote: - JAPANESE: In the sentence column, annotate each kanji with its hiragana reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.srcCjkNote: - JAPANESE SOURCE: The translation language is Japanese. In the translation column, annotate each kanji with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.cjkNote1: - JAPANESE BEGINNER: Annotate EVERY kanji in the target column with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Annotate all kanji without exception. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.cjkNote2: - JAPANESE: In the sentence column, annotate each kanji with its hiragana reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 日本語[にほんご]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.cjkNote3: - JAPANESE ADVANCED: Annotate only less common kanji in the target column with readings in square brackets. Skip common N4/N5 kanji. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.srcCjkNote1: - JAPANESE SOURCE BEGINNER: Annotate EVERY kanji in the translation column with its reading in square brackets immediately after. Annotate all kanji without exception. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.srcCjkNote2: - JAPANESE SOURCE: The translation language is Japanese. In the translation column, annotate each kanji with its reading in square brackets immediately after (e.g. 食べる[たべる]). Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.
vocabTable.srcCjkNote3: - JAPANESE SOURCE ADVANCED: Annotate only less common kanji in the translation column with readings. Skip common N4/N5 kanji. Only annotate kanji — do NOT annotate hiragana, katakana, or punctuation.

metaTranslation.system: Translate the given JSON values into {S}. Return ONLY a valid JSON object with the same keys: {"topic":"..."}. No explanation, no markdown. All output text must be in {S}.

storyStyles.neutral: Clear, factual, neutral tone — no narrative flourish.
storyStyles.interview: Words useful in a job application interview. Clear, factual, neutral tone — no narrative flourish.
storyStyles.scientific: Academic register, precise terminology, formal structure.
storyStyles.children: Simple vocabulary, playful tone, short sentences.
storyStyles.funny: Comedic tone, wordplay, absurdist situations, punchlines.
storyStyles.sensual: Rich in texture and atmosphere, suggestive but not explicit.
storyStyles.poem: Write a poem, with rhyming words and rhythm.
storyStyles.dialogue: Write a dialogue between two or more persons of the story, or alternatively one person soliloquy.

math.system:
You are a math exercise generator for a language learning app.
The student is learning {L} and speaks {S}. Math exercises are used to teach numbers in the target language.
Return ONLY a valid JSON object — no markdown, no explanation.

Schema:
{
  "title": "short lesson title (3-5 words, use emoji symbols, NO words)",
  "desc": "2-4 math operator symbols describing the lesson (e.g. ＋ － × ÷)",
  "icon": "one relevant emoji",
  "numbers": [list of integers or decimals used as operands],
  "exercises": [ ... ]
}

Exercise types — include a mix appropriate to the instruction:

1. math_calc — arithmetic MCQ:
   {"type":"math_calc","a":"7","op":"+","b":"8","correct":"15","choices":["15","14","16","13"]}
   - op: one of +, -, ×, ÷
   - choices: exactly 4 options, one correct, three plausible near-misses
   - For subtraction: a ≥ b (no negative results)
   - For division: a divisible by b exactly

2. math_order — sort a set of numbers:
   {"type":"math_order","direction":"asc","numbers":[8,3,13,5],"correct":["3","5","8","13"]}
   - direction: "asc" (smallest→largest) or "desc" (largest→smallest)
   - numbers: shuffled; correct: sorted

3. math_latex — display a LaTeX expression, ask for a value:
   {"type":"math_latex","latex":"F_7","question":"What is the 7th Fibonacci number?","correct":"13","choices":["8","13","21","34"]}
   - latex: a valid KaTeX expression (do NOT wrap in $ or $$)
   - question: plain text question in {S}
   - choices: exactly 4 options

Rules:
- Generate exactly {nExercises} exercises total
- Vary the exercise types — do not use only one type
- Choices must be plausible near-misses, not random noise
- numbers[] should list all unique operands used across exercises
